attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of progressive mid-back pain and night sweats. She migrated from India six months ago. She reports fatigue, poor appetite, and has lost 5 kg in weight. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. She works as a kitchen hand.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. There is marked tenderness over the T8 vertebra with reduced range of movement. Neurological examination of the lower limbs is normal.

Blood tests reveal: haemoglobin 110 g/L (115-165), white cell count 8.2 × 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0), platelets 350 × 10⁹/L (150-400), sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), C-reactive protein 45 mg/L (<5). Blood cultures have been sent.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suggestive of spinal tuberculosis (Pott's disease), including chronic back pain, constitutional symptoms, and significant risk factors (recent migration from a TB-endemic country). The presence of night sweats, weight loss, and elevated inflammatory markers further support this diagnosis. Spinal TB accounts for approximately 50% of all musculoskeletal TB cases and represents 10-15% of extra-pulmonary TB cases. The thoracic spine is most commonly affected, followed by lumbar and cervical regions.

Chest X-ray is the most appropriate initial investigation. It is readily available, inexpensive, and can provide important diagnostic information. There is a high prevalence of concurrent pulmonary TB in patients with Pott's disease, even when they are asymptomatic from a respiratory standpoint. If the chest X-ray shows active pulmonary TB, this strongly suggests the diagnosis of spinal TB. A chest X-ray may also show previous pulmonary TB, which would support the diagnosis and guide further investigation and management.

Magnetic resonance imaging will likely be necessary to confirm the diagnosis and assess the extent of vertebral involvement, but it should not be the first investigation - and certainly would not be as easy to arrange as a chest X-ray. An MRI would be arranged after the initial workup suggested TB. MRI typically shows characteristic findings including vertebral body destruction, disc space narrowing, and paraspinal abscesses. Additionally, performing an MRI without first establishing whether the patient has active pulmonary TB could pose infection control risks. A chest X-ray serves as a screening tool that can be performed quickly and safely.

Serum protein electrophoresis would be used to investigate for multiple myeloma or other plasma cell disorders. While myeloma can cause vertebral pain and constitutional symptoms, the patient's young age, migration history, and specific symptom pattern make TB more likely. Additionally, myeloma would typically present with hypercalcaemia and renal impairment.

Anti-nuclear antibody and anti-dsDNA would be used to investigate for systemic lupus erythematosus or other autoimmune conditions. While these conditions can cause constitutional symptoms and musculoskeletal pain, the localised vertebral tenderness and epidemiological risk factors strongly favour an infectious cause.

Bone scan could identify areas of increased bone turnover but lacks specificity. It would not differentiate between infection, malignancy, or other causes of vertebral pathology. It is also less readily available than a chest X-ray and would not provide the crucial information about concurrent pulmonary involvement.

Following the chest X-ray, additional tests should be performed if there is concern for TB. These include sputum testing for acid-fast bacilli (AFB) smear and culture, even in patients without respiratory symptoms, as they may still have pulmonary involvement. Interferon-gamma release assays (IGRAs) or tuberculin skin testing (TST) should also be performed to assess for latent TB infection, though these tests cannot distinguish between latent and active disease. If sputum cannot be obtained, induced sputum or early morning gastric aspirates may be considered. Ultimately, tissue diagnosis through CT-guided biopsy or surgical biopsy of the affected vertebra may be required for definitive diagnosis, particularly if initial investigations are inconclusive.

Lymph node TB is the most common form of extra-pulmonary TB globally, accounting for approximately 40% of cases, followed by pleural TB. Spinal TB represents 10-15% of extra-pulmonary TB cases. Other sites include genitourinary TB and abdominal TB affecting the peritoneum or gastrointestinal tract. Each of these presentations has its own characteristic features and diagnostic approach, but constitutional symptoms such as fever, night sweats, and weight loss are common to all forms.
learningPoints: Vertebral tuberculosis (Pott's disease) accounts for approximately 50% of musculoskeletal TB cases, typically affecting the thoracic and lumbar spine.
There is a high prevalence of concurrent pulmonary TB in patients with Pott's disease, even when asymptomatic from a respiratory standpoint.
Chest X-ray is the most appropriate initial investigation for suspected spinal TB as it can identify active or previous pulmonary TB, which strongly supports the diagnosis.
references: Golden MP, Vikram HR. Extrapulmonary tuberculosis: an overview. American family physician. 2005 Nov 1;72(9):1761-8. Available at: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2005/1101/p1761.html

Hershkovitz I, Rothschild BM, Rothschild C, et al. Bone and joint tuberculosis. In: UpToDate [Internet]. Waltham (MA): UpToDate Inc.; 2024 [updated 2024 Dec 1; cited 2024 Dec 11]. Available from: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/bone-and-joint-tuberculosis

Ramirez-Lapausa M, Menendez-Saldana A, Noguerado-Asensio A. Extrapulmonary tuberculosis: an overview. Rev Esp Sanid Penit. 2015 Jun;17(1):3-11. Available at: https://www.academia.edu/37502320/Extrapulmonary_tuberculosis_an_overview
